How the revenant is named before its grave is proven
How the werewolf is named before its host is proven
Grimoire entry body text
english: [Some evils are inherited like debts. Where the trouble is old, its signs are old: parish records, weathered stones, dreams that repeat in different sleepers. Ask the church and the graves before you ask the living.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Grimoire entry body text
english: [Desperate men with pistols and poor judgement. They keep their distance and shoot while they can; close with them, or answer in kind. Ask who pays them, for someone always does.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Grimoire entry body text
english: [Grave-dust freely given, moon-herb picked by night, sewn tight in a caul. Pressed close to the risen dead it strips their shroud of shadow for five long breaths — long enough, for a hunter who has kept her steel ready.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Grimoire entry body text
english: [Some evils are minted fresh from a wrong done between the living. Where the trouble is new, its signs walk about the settlement: grudges nursed, names not spoken, blood not yet dry. Ask the living, and watch their eyes.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Grimoire entry body text
english: [The hardest kind to read, because it wears the other two like borrowed coats. An obligation kept faithfully for generations — a payment, a rite, a door left unlocked one night a year — and then, within living memory, somebody decided it was superstition and stopped. It looks like an ancient curse, because the compact is genuinely old. It looks like a bitter betrayal, because a person alive today did the breaking. What tells you the truth is the *terms*: a curse rages, a grudge accuses, but a broken covenant simply presents the account and waits. Find the ledger, find the year the line was ruled through, and you have it. What answers it is the compact's own seal.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Grimoire entry body text
english: [Iron dug from the bog and beaten cold, never once shown the fire. Every working needs purchase, some edge or surface to take hold of, and unsmelted iron offers none — a hex slides off it the way water slides off wax. Against a warded thing it is not a better blade, it is the only blade. Draw the pin at the forge like any other work, but keep it cold, and quench it in what the evil's beginning demands.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Grimoire entry body text
english: [A church may be made proof against the risen dead: a rite of one evening, if the altar is willing. On warded ground a revenant's shroud cannot close and the very floor scalds it. Against a living curse like the beast, the rite is so much candle-smoke. Choose the ground of your last stand as carefully as your weapon.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Grimoire entry body text
english: [Some monsters kill only as a mason chips stone: in service of a work. Watch for hired men guarding nothing worth stealing, and desecrations that look like errands. Each stage the design completes, its author grows greater.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Grimoire entry body text
english: [A counter is only half made when it leaves the forge. Every decisive work must be quenched in something that answers *how the evil began*, and the three beginnings demand three different things. What was cursed in the deep past yields to what the parish blessed in the deep past: hallowed water from the font. What was born of a wrong done between the living yields to earth from the wronged one's own plot, because a debt answers to the one it was owed. What was a compact broken yields to the compact's own seal, the promise and the withholding together in one piece of wax. Read the beginning before you light the forge. You can carry all three and be certain — but you will pay for the certainty in daylight, and daylight is the one thing you cannot craft.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Grimoire entry body text
english: [A snare set in a doorway has killed more monsters than any sword. A draught of moon-herb has saved more hunters than any prayer. Take the hour at the forge; the grave you dodge may be your own.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Grimoire entry body text
english: [Some monsters kill to be feared, and grow strong on the fearing. Each outrage it lands unanswered, it swells bolder and harder to put down. Do not give it the week it wants.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Grimoire entry body text
english: [Corpses walked by another's will. Slow — mercifully slow — but tireless, and strong enough to matter if you let them lay hands on you. Where the restless dead shuffle, a greater dead commands.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Grimoire entry body text
english: [A corpse that rose with unfinished business and no intention of leaving it so. It sleeps in its own grave between errands. No blade nor ball bites it while its shroud of grave-shadow holds; the shroud thins for a moment at its own slow rhythm — you will see it, and it knows you can. It flows across ground unnaturally fast when it wishes, and it wishes it mostly when retreating from your steel or closing on your throat. Consecrated ground holds the shroud open and scalds it; a binding charm pressed close does the same for a handful of breaths. Learn the rhythm, or bring the counter.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Grimoire entry body text
english: [True silver is the old wound the curse remembers. A ball of it, cast honest over a forge, bites the beast to the quick — and what silver tears, no moon can mend. Churches keep more silver than they know; so do abandoned mines.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Grimoire entry body text
english: [It does not hunt. It invites. One by one the valley folk get up in the night, unlatch their own doors, and walk out unhurried along a road they have never chosen — and something at the far end keeps the tally. You will find no struggle and no blood, only even-strided footprints leading away, and stones set along the trail scored with the same mark and placed each within sight of the next. That is a road built to be followed by someone not deciding to follow it. Throw the route-stones down and grind the marks off, and the ones who walk out that night will come to themselves cold and bewildered at the trail's edge, and the tally will fall short.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Grimoire entry body text
english: [Those who answered the call and kept walking. They come at you steadily and without malice, and their eyes pass over your face without finding anything in it. They can be put down like anything else, and it is worth remembering while you do it that a week ago one of them sold you bread.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Grimoire entry body text
english: [Every night you spend certain-but-unready, the enemy spends feeding. An evil that lands its outrages grows in hide and cunning both. The prepared early hunt is the master's stroke; the patient hunt is the journeyman's; the hunt on the final night is the one you did not choose.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Grimoire entry body text
english: [By day it wears a neighbour's face and does a neighbour's work; you will not know it by looking. By night it is appetite in a grey hide. Its wounds close as fast as you can deal them — save one kind. Watch for the gather-and-leap: given a clear run it will cross half a field in a heartbeat, so keep a tree, a cart, a wall between you and it. Break its lane and you break its favourite trick. Two honest proofs of its day-name are enough to hunt it; one is gossip, and gossip hangs innocents.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Grimoire entry body text
english: [Not a hag on a heath — a neighbour, with a trade and a doorstep and an opinion about the weather. She keeps the old compacts and asks the old prices, and the working she wears turns honest steel aside as if the air had gone thick. You will know her by her hand: hex-work is *drawn*, cut deliberate into lintels and thresholds, and a beast cannot draw and the dead do not need to. Do not go at her with good steel and confidence. The hex has to get hold of a thing to turn it, and it can get no hold at all on cold iron — bog iron, unsmelted, never near the forge fire. Wear the hex down or cut straight through it; either way, be carrying the pin.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Grimoire entry body text
english: [Honest beasts, if hungry ones. They come straight on and die like anything else. When wolves grow bold near a settlement, ask what emboldened them.]

context: Grimoire entry title
Guide entry body, shown when the entry is selected
english: [Every case has a beginning, and the beginning decides what your weapon must be quenched in. An old curse answers to what the parish blessed long ago. A wrong done between the living answers to earth from the wronged one's own plot. A broken compact answers to the seal that sealed it.|Read the beginning before you light the forge. You can carry all three reagents and be certain — but you will pay for that certainty in daylight, and daylight is the one thing you cannot craft.]

context: Guide entry title, listed on the guide screen
Guide entry body, shown when the entry is selected
english: [There is always a second way through. Every case is built so that losing an informant, or a route, or a fight, leaves another road open — you will not be shut out of a case by one bad afternoon.|Attacking a villager is the exception. Do that and the settlement closes to you, prices rise, and whatever that person knew goes with them. Be certain before you draw on somebody with a face.]

context: Guide entry title, listed on the guide screen
Guide entry body, shown when the entry is selected
english: [You cannot hunt what you have not named. Naming takes two proofs, and at least one of them must rule something out.|A sign that merely agrees with your suspicion is not enough, however many you gather: three villagers all saying it felt wrong is three of the same nothing. What you need is the sign that says it cannot be the other thing — tracks that turn into bootprints, steel that turned aside, ground nobody has disturbed. Look for the clue that eliminates, and take the corroborating one on the way past.]

context: Guide entry title, listed on the guide screen
Guide entry body, shown when the entry is selected
english: [Travelling between the settlement, the wood and the outlying site costs a day. Nothing else does — walking about within one place is free, and so is talking, looking, and thinking.|So the expensive decision is never which clue to take. It is which map to be standing on. Plan your day around the trip, and do everything a place owes you before you leave it.]

context: Guide entry title, listed on the guide screen
Guide entry body, shown when the entry is selected
english: [Each kind of thing has one kind of death. A beast that mends itself needs silver. A hexed neighbour needs cold iron, dug from a bog and never near a fire. The risen dead need consecrated ground or a charm to strip their shadow.|Gather the makings, take them to a forge, and quench the work in what the beginning demands. Go to the final night without the counter in your pack and you are not hunting; you are visiting.]

context: Guide entry title, listed on the guide screen
Guide entry body, shown when the entry is selected
english: [When you have the name and the counter, go to it. A thing asleep in its grave takes the first blow for free, and that blow is worth more than any other you will land. A thing wearing a neighbour's face must be confronted where it stands.|Watch what it telegraphs. Nothing in this valley kills you without first telling you it is about to.]

context: Guide entry title, listed on the guide screen
Guide entry body, shown when the entry is selected
english: [Six days, three places, one thing killing people. You win by proving what it is, preparing the one thing that kills it, and then going to where it sleeps. You lose by running out of days. Everything else is detail.|Nothing you learn is ever lost. Dying costs you a day and nothing else — not your findings, not your pack. Days are the only thing you cannot get back, so spend them on work that narrows the answer.]

context: Guide entry title, listed on the guide screen
Guide entry body, shown when the entry is selected
english: [The thing in the valley is not merely feeding; it is doing something, and on the fourth day it will do more of it. Find the sign that says which, and you will know where to go to blunt it before it lands.|Pre-empting costs you a trip and a point. Not pre-empting costs you a stronger quarry on the last night. Neither is wrong, but choose on purpose.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [It is a soft winter, all mud and no frost, and the valley has the sulky look of a place that would rather be under snow.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [You misjudged the road and come in well after dark, with no moon worth the name. You will be working close tonight, and close is where the disadvantage is.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [It is an open winter, the roads hard and clear the length of the valley. You can get about, which is not nothing — most of this trade is getting about.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [You come in at dusk, with the light going amber and then going altogether, and the valley folding itself up for the night rather faster than it needs to.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [Somebody here has done this before: a room held for you, and on the table a stoppered bottle of the right sort, put by without being asked for and without anyone taking the credit.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [You come in at first light, which is the honest hour to arrive somewhere — early enough to be seen doing it, late enough not to wake anybody.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [You are barely a fortnight out of the last one, and it has not finished with you yet. You still check doorways. You will do that for a while.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [High summer, and too hot for the time of year. The stock are in the shade and the flies are at everything, and the smell reaches you well before {place} does.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [Nobody much minds that you are here. That is worse than suspicion, in its way — a valley this used to bad news has been getting it a while.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [Somebody here has heard of you, and has told the rest. The version that reached them is flattering and wrong, and you will be corrected out of it by about Thursday.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [They are glad to see you, openly and a little desperately, and you have learned to distrust that. Glad people expect to be saved on a schedule.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [It is the deep of winter, the part with no give in it, and the light gone by four. Everything out here is hungry, and hungry things come further down the valley than they otherwise would.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [You come in at the flat middle of the day, when a place has nothing to hide behind and shows you exactly how it is doing. It is not doing well.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [You did not come here cleanly: there is a matter two valleys back that involves people prepared to travel. They will not come into the settlement, but the roads between are theirs as much as anyone's.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [The thaw is on. Everything is running — ditches, gutters, the fields themselves — and the whole valley sounds like something being poured away.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [You come in with the whole of the day still in front of you, and the light as good as it gets here — which is not very, but it is honest. You will see what there is to see.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [Word went ahead of you and it was not kind — a stranger come to make trouble out of a run of hard luck, decided before you opened your mouth. Everything you want from these people you will pay more for, and nobody will sell you so much as powder.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [They are wary of you, which is sensible of them. Doors do not shut as you pass, but they are held, and the holding is the message.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [Nobody here has heard of you, which is restful. You get to be exactly as good as you turn out to be, with nothing to live up to and nothing to live down.]

context: Valley-condition paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [You came in with everything where it should be: powder dry, ball counted, nothing left behind at the last place. A small thing to be pleased about, and small things are most of what a hunter gets.]

context: Opening narration paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [You did not come into the valley empty-handed. There had been one stop on the way in, and it had been worth making.]

context: Opening narration paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [{npc} met you on the way in and gave you a direction, which is more than most will give a stranger and considerably less than you are going to need.]

context: Opening narration paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [Nobody sent for you; you heard it third-hand at a market forty miles off, a valley losing people and calling it accidents. So you have no fee and no authority — but whatever is here has not been warned.]

context: Opening narration paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [{clue} caught your eye on the way in — not enough to stop for long, enough to know where you will be going back to.]

context: Opening narration paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [You were going somewhere else. You are not going there now: one is a story, two is a coincidence, three is a pattern, and you have never once been wrong about the third thing.]

context: Opening narration paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [{npc} had business with you before you were properly arrived, and you came away from it carrying something.]

context: Opening narration paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [You stopped at {clue} before you were properly arrived, on the sound principle that a hunter who walks past the first strange thing is shown the second at a worse moment. What you read there is in your head now, and the valley cannot unsay it.]

context: Opening narration paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [The letter reached you at Wenlock, folded twice and written in three hands. It says: come. It does not say what for — that much they will tell you to your face, or not at all.]

context: Opening narration paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [{npc} found you at the ford before you found the valley, said it all in a rush, and was away up the bank before you could ask a second question. It is not proof — but it is yours already, and nothing that happens here can take it back out of your head.]

context: Opening narration paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [There was a hunter here before you, and the parish is vague about what became of him in the particular way that means they know exactly. His kit is still in the inn's back room, and nobody will meet your eye about it.]

context: Opening narration paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [The parish put up the money, which means the parish has decided this is real, and has known it rather longer than anyone will admit to your face. They will be polite, and helpful, and not quick.]

context: Opening narration paragraph, shown as the run begins
english: [You came in along the drove road at first light, past a byre with the door off its hinges and nothing living inside it. Whatever walks here has been walking a while.]

context: Flavour shown when preparing the reagent that counters this origin

context: Origin description, shown in the case report and grimoire

context: Origin description, shown in the case report and grimoire

context: Name of a case origin, shown once proven

context: Origin description, shown in the case report and grimoire

context: Scheme description, shown in the case report and grimoire
